statement: The EU acquis communautaire is organised into 35 negotiating chapters for accession purposes, covering everything from free movement of goods (Chapter 1) to 'other issues' (Chapter 35). The chapters were expanded from 31 (used for the 2004/2007 enlargements) to 35 for Croatia's accession in 2013 to allow better balance between topics. Estimates of the acquis's size vary: the European Commission historically cited approximately 80,000–100,000 pages of legislative text, while a 2023 EUR-Lex analysis found that the EU had passed over 666,000 pages of law since 1957, of which roughly 26% (approximately 170,000 pages) remain in force. The acquis includes treaties, regulations, directives, decisions, recommendations, and CJEU case law. The volume continues to grow each year as new legislation is adopted.
source_name: European Commission — Chapters of the Acquis; EUR-Lex legislative database
caveats: The '100,000 pages' figure frequently cited in Icelandic debate is a rough estimate from the early 2000s. The actual volume depends on counting methodology (active vs repealed, primary vs delegated acts, with or without case law). The acquis has grown substantially since that estimate was made.

related_entries.statement: As of late 2025, approximately 13,000 EU legal acts (directives and regulations) had been incorporated into the EEA Agreement and transposed into Icelandic law since the Agreement entered into force in 1994. This represents the cumulative acquis adopted by Iceland through the EEA Joint Committee decision process.

The EEA Agreement covers the EU's four freedoms (goods, services, capital, persons) and flanking policies (competition, state aid, public procurement, company law, environment, social policy). It explicitly excludes the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP), Common Fisheries Policy (CFP), customs union, common trade policy, common foreign and security policy, and justice/home affairs.

citations.report_title: Ræða þarf alla kaflana á nýjan leik

claims.claim_text: Hefjist aðildarviðræður á nýjan leik þarf að ræða alla 33 kaflana aftur þar sem löggjöf bæði ESB og Íslands hefur breyst frá 2013.
